text.title: Experiment Hypothesis Scoring Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of Experiment Hypothesis Scoring must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every level on the scale has at least one concrete behavioural anchor that can be observed in the last 90 days.

rationale.source: Anti-vague-rubric practice from clinical/educational measurement (Stevens, Level

rule.rationale: Anti-vague-rubric practice from clinical/educational measurement (Stevens, Levels of Measurement, 1946).

rule.statement: Scores require ≥1 piece of named evidence (quote, ticket, dashboard row). Numeric scores without evidence default to the lowest level.
rule.rationale: scores without traceable evidence reduce to political negotiation.

rule.statement: Inter-rater calibration: every rubric run has ≥2 raters; discrepancies >1 band trigger a third pass with discussion.
Rubric is frozen for the duration of the review window. Mid-cycle rubric edits invalidate the cycle.
rule.rationale: moving the ruler invalidates the scoreboard.

example.title: Anchored application
example.code: Trigger: Synthesis: Weekly CRO experiment cadence on a primary landing page Artefact: structured output meeting the contract in content/02-output-contract.xml Evidence: ≥1 named source per rule (URL, ticket, transcript_id) Decision: downstream consumer can act without re-deriving reasoning
example.note: Demonstrates the closed loop the methodology creates — trigger → artefact → decision — with auditability at each step.

example.title: Un-anchored application
example.code: Trigger: 'we should do this thing' Artefact: prose paragraph, no schema Evidence: 'industry best practice' Decision: ambiguous — every reader interprets differently
example.note: Each ambiguity above is a failure mode catalogued in content/03-failure-modes.xml. The methodology exists to prevent exactly this shape.
